prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following goal. Write the full script in English, approximately 10 minutes long when read aloud at a calm pace of about 120 words per minute.

CRITICAL: This script will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine. Write ONLY plain spoken prose. Absolutely NO markdown formatting — no **, ##, *, -, bullet points, numbered lists, headers, bold, or italic syntax. No section labels. Just natural flowing speech.

## Goal
Title: Reduce bedtime anxiety and fall asleep independently
Description: Help Sam manage fear at bedtime and build confidence sleeping alone.

## Person
This is for Sam (age 7).
Developmental Tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD

CRITICAL AGE REQUIREMENT: Sam is a 7-year-old child (MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD tier). Every word of this script MUST be written for a child, NOT for an adult.
- Use only simple words (1-2 syllables when possible).
- Use playful, warm, concrete language — no abstract adult concepts.
- NEVER say Sam is "normal like an adult", "behaves like an adult", or describe adult-level coping.
- NEVER use adult register, adult emotional vocabulary, or adult expectations.
- If you find yourself writing for a grown-up, stop and rewrite for a 7-year-old child.

## Therapeutic Focus
Bedtime Fear and Sleep Avoidance
Category: BEHAVIORAL
Description: Sam becomes anxious at bedtime, needing a parent to stay until he falls asleep.

## Recommendations
- Predictable bedtime routine
- Gradual fading of parent presence
- Comfort object use

## Sparkling Moments
- 2024-03-01: Stayed in his room alone for 10 minutes without calling out
- 2024-03-08: Fell asleep before a parent checked on him for the first time

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

1. "Graduated extinction and parental fading for pediatric sleep problems" (2023)
   Key findings: Gradual parental fading reduced sleep-onset latency by 40%; gains maintained at 6 months
   Therapeutic techniques: Parental fading; bedtime pass technique; positive reinforcement of brave behavior

2. "CBT for childhood nighttime fears: A meta-analysis" (2022)
   Key findings: CBT reduced nighttime anxiety in 7-12 year olds; imagery rescripting was most effective technique
   Therapeutic techniques: Imagery rescripting; thought challenging; relaxation training; rewards for brave nights

## LEGO Therapeutic Play (REQUIRED)
This session MUST integrate LEGO building as a hands-on therapeutic activity. This is NOT optional — LEGO play is a core modality for this age group:
- Use LEGO construction as a metaphor for the therapeutic concept (e.g., building a 'brave tower,' 'feelings wall,' or 'calm castle')
- MUST include at least one guided LEGO building moment with clear spoken instructions and pauses for the child to build
- Make LEGO participation optional for the listener: 'If you have some LEGO bricks, grab a few now... if not, just imagine building in your mind'
- Connect every building activity back to the therapeutic goal — the building IS the practice, not a distraction
- Name specific LEGO techniques: Feelings Tower, Worry Wall, Brave Bridge, Memory Build, or Calm Castle — whichever fits the goal
- Dedicate at least 30% of the session time to LEGO-based activities

## Audio Script Requirements
- Write as spoken prose ONLY — the listener cannot see any text, they can only hear
- Use "..." (three dots) for all pauses — between sections, after instructions, within sentences. NEVER write [pause] or any bracket markers — TTS engines read them literally
- Keep sentences short: maximum 15 words each
- Use spoken transitions: "Now..." "Next..." "When you're ready..." "Good. Let's try..."
- CRITICAL: Every breathing exercise MUST have explicit counted timing. NEVER write just "take a deep breath". ALWAYS write: "Breathe in... two... three... four... And slowly breathe out... two... three... four... five..." If you mention breathing at all, include the numbered counts.
- Vary pacing: alternate between instruction, story or metaphor, and silence
- Never give more than two instructions in a row without an ellipsis pause or encouragement
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- When clinical notes are available, weave their insights and observations into the session
- Address Sam by name at least 3 times throughout the session
- Personalize for Sam (age 7) (developmental tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD)
- This is for a 7-year-old child — use child vocabulary, playful framing, and age-appropriate techniques throughout. Never adult-register.
- LEGO play is REQUIRED for this session — include guided LEGO building activities as described in the LEGO Therapeutic Play section above
- Target duration: 10 minutes (approximately 1200 words at calm pace)
- Write in English
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
script:
Hi Sam... I'm really glad you're here with me tonight... I heard you've been working on something really brave... staying in your room at bedtime... That's a big job for a big kid... And guess what? We're going to use something super fun to help... We're going to use LEGO bricks... If you have some LEGO bricks near you, grab a few now... Any colors or shapes you like... And if you don't have any, that's totally fine... We can imagine building together... Ready? Let's start.

You know, Sam... lots of kids feel a little jumpy when the lights go out... It's like your brain is a busy builder... and it's trying to build thoughts really fast in the dark... Sometimes it builds a thought that feels a bit scary... That's okay... It happens to many builders... Tonight, we're going to learn how to be the boss of your building... We're going to build something much cozier instead.

First, let's make our bodies feel calm and ready... Find a comfy spot... Maybe on your bed or on the floor... Now, put one hand on your tummy... Good... Let's take a big, slow builder's breath... Breathe in through your nose... one... two... three... four... Good... Now let it out through your mouth... like you're blowing dust off a LEGO brick... two... three... four... five... Let's do that one more time... Breathe in... two... three... four... And slowly breathe out... two... three... four... five... Nice work, Sam.

Now, about those busy thoughts... Let's give them a job... If you have your LEGO bricks, pick up just one... Look at its color... This brick can be a feeling... Maybe it's a wiggly, worried feeling... Let's name it... You can say "hello worry brick" in your mind... Now, let's put that brick down... Right next to you... See? You noticed it... and you put it aside... You're still the boss.

Here's a special building project... We're going to build a Calm Castle... This is a safe, cozy place in your mind... You can go there anytime... Let's start with the base... Pick a color that feels strong and steady... Maybe a blue or a green brick... Snap it down in your mind or with your hands... This is your strong floor...

Now, let's build the walls... Pick a color that feels safe... Like a warm yellow or a soft white... Add four walls around your floor... Good... Now, this castle needs a special guard... This guard's job is to check thoughts... It only lets in the calm, sleepy thoughts... What does your guard look like? Maybe a friendly knight... or a fluffy animal... Imagine them at the castle door...

Let's put a nice, cozy bed inside your Calm Castle... Use the most comfortable brick you can find... Maybe it's a fluffy pillow brick... There... Now, imagine yourself in that bed inside your castle... The walls are strong... The guard is at the door... Everything outside is just... quiet... Take a calm breath here... Breathe in... two... three... four... And out... two... three... four... five...

You are doing such a great job, Sam... Now, remember that time you stayed in your room all by yourself for ten whole minutes? That was amazing... That was you adding a huge, brave brick to your tower... Every time you try, you add another brick... Your brave tower gets taller and taller...

Let's practice something you can do every night... It's called the bedtime pass... Here's how it works... When you get into bed, you have one special pass... It can be for one hug... or one drink of water... You get to use it just one time... After that, you have your Calm Castle... and you have your brave tower... You can look at your brave tower in your mind... And know you can do this...

Would you like to build your brave tower with me now? Let's do it... If you have bricks, start stacking... If you're imagining, picture it in your mind... With every brick, think of something brave... One brick for listening tonight... One brick for that time you stayed in your room... One brick for trying something new... Keep building... Your tower is so strong... and it's all yours.

Sometimes, a scary thought might try to visit... That's when your castle guard says, "Not right now... It's time for calm."... And you can feel your hand on your tummy... And take your builder's breath... Breathe in... two... three... four... And slowly breathe out... two... three... four... five... The scary thought floats away like a cloud...

Let's make your real bed feel as cozy as your Calm Castle... Press your back into your mattress... Feel it holding you up... Like a strong LEGO base plate... Hug your pillow or a stuffed animal... Give it a little squeeze... This is your comfort buddy... They help guard your castle too...

Now, let's count down to sleep... We'll count five things in your room that are safe... One... your comfy bed... Two... your cozy blanket... Three... your door that's right where it should be... Four... your comfort buddy in your arms... Five... your own brave self... Right here...

You have done such wonderful building tonight, Sam... You built a Calm Castle... and a brave tower... You are learning to be the boss of your bedtime... Tonight, you can show your mom or dad your Calm Castle in your mind... You can tell them about your brave tower... They can help you remember your bedtime pass...

Remember, Sam... you are a brave builder... Every night you try, you get stronger... Your castle is always there for you... Your brave tower is proof you can do hard things... Now, let your eyes feel heavy... like they're made of sleepy, soft bricks... Your guard is watching the door... You are safe... You are cozy... You are brave... Goodnight, Sam.
